entry.title: Re: SAP FI – Document Currency Not Zero but Local Currenc...
entry.content:
Hi,

For foreign currency revaluation of non-open items, the valuation is performed at the balance level, not at the individual document level.

Therefore, when the foreign currency balance is zero, but a local currency balance still exists, the system will nevertheless select the account for revaluation. In such cases, even though the document currency amount is zero, the local currency amount remains and is subject to valuation.

Ideally, when the balance in the document (foreign) currency is zero, the corresponding balance in the company code currency should also be zero. However, if this is not the case, the system performs a valuation adjustment to align the local currency balance accordingly.

This behavior is explained in SAP Note 2406391 – FAGL_FCV Zero Balance Valuation.

As per the SAP note:

For G/L balance sheet accounts, if the foreign currency balance is zero but the local currency balance still has a value (for example, FC = 0 and LC = 180), the system translates the zero foreign currency balance to zero in local currency. To achieve this result, the valuation posting adjusts the local currency amount (e.g., posting –180) so that the final balance in local currency also becomes zero.

I hope this clarifies the issue.

Regards,
Ayush Kumar Jain

entry.title: Clean Core Compliance – Handling Dynamic WHERE and Dynamic Table Names in ABAP
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

We are currently working on aligning our custom ABAP developments with Clean Core / ABAP Cloud guidelines, and we encountered ATC warnings related to the following patterns:

  1. Usage of dynamic WHERE conditions in Open SQL
  2. Usage of dynamic table/view names in Open SQL

As per Clean Core principles, these constructs are discouraged due to potential concerns around security, stability, and upgrade safety.

Our Current Approach

To address these warnings while preserving required flexibility, we implemented validation using standard class methods:

  • cl_abap_dyn_prg=>check_column_name( ) — for validating dynamic WHERE clause fields
  • cl_abap_dyn_prg=>check_table_or_view_name_str( ) — for validating dynamic table/view names

After introducing these validations, the ATC warnings are no longer triggered.

Questions

We would appreciate guidance from the community on the following:

  1. Compliance Clarification
    Is the use of CL_ABAP_DYN_PRG validation methods considered fully compliant with Clean Core principles, or does it merely act as a workaround to suppress ATC warnings?
  2. Recommended Approach for Dynamic Scenarios
    In cases where dynamic behavior is functionally required (e.g., configurable providers, metadata-driven frameworks, generic handlers), what is SAP’s recommended best practice under Clean Core guidelines?
  3. Alternative APIs / Frameworks
    Are there any released and upgrade-safe APIs or frameworks (especially in ABAP Cloud) that can replace such dynamic constructs?

Objective

Our goal is not just to suppress warnings but to ensure that our implementation aligns with SAP’s long-term vision for upgrade-safe, extensible, and compliant development.

Any insights, official documentation references, SAP Notes, or practical recommendations would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support!
Best regards,
Sunil

entry.title: From Securing Systems to Securing Decisions: The New Reality of Agentic AI
entry.content:
(Jana Subramanian serves as the APJ Principal Cybersecurity and Compliance Advisor, SEA. In this role, Jana supports strategic customer engagements on topics such as AI security and governance, cloud security, data privacy, secure data flows, multi-cloud security integration architecture, contractual assurance, audit, and compliance.)

A security model built for systems is now being tested by autonomous decisions

For years, enterprise security has been built around a familiar model: secure the infrastructure, protect identities, control network access, harden applications, and monitor data movement. That model served the cloud era well because most enterprise applications behaved in predictable ways. Developers defined the logic, and software executed it.

Agentic AI changes that assumption.

An AI agent does not simply process a request. It interprets language, reasons at runtime, invokes tools, connects across systems, and increasingly takes action with a degree of autonomy. That is why this is not just another extension of cloud security. It is a fundamental shift in the security model.

Why traditional cloud security is no longer enough on its own

Traditional cloud security was designed for a world where humans were the primary decision-makers and software was the execution layer. In that world, the key questions were straightforward:

  • Who has access to what?
  • Which network paths are exposed?
  • Which APIs are vulnerable?
  • Where is sensitive data stored or transmitted?

These questions still matter. But with agentic AI, they are no longer sufficient.

We must now also ask:

  • What is the agent being told?
  • How does it interpret an instruction?
  • Which tools is it allowed to invoke?
  • Should it act autonomously at all?

The risk is no longer only about access to systems. It is also about influence over the agent’s decision-making.

The attack surface has changed in a fundamental way

A firewall can inspect traffic. IAM can enforce permissions. DLP can monitor sensitive data movement. These remain essential controls, but they were not designed for a world where a well-crafted sentence becomes the attack vector.

In agentic AI, language itself becomes part of the attack surface. Prompt injection, instruction manipulation, unsafe tool chaining, excessive autonomy, and context abuse reflect a very different threat model from classical cloud applications.

 

In traditional software, code was the mechanism. In agentic AI, language can also become the mechanism of attack.

That is the tectonic shift.

What enterprises now need to secure

Agentic AI security requires a broader control model around how the agent receives, interprets, and acts on information.

This includes:

  • content filtering to detect unsafe or manipulative instructions
  • data masking to reduce exposure of sensitive information in prompts and responses
  • prompt templating to keep interactions structured and bounded
  • orchestration controls with least-privilege access to tools
  • human approval for high-risk actions
  • runtime isolation to prevent cross-tenant leakage
  • auditability for forensic visibility and accountability

These are no longer optional enhancements. They are becoming core design requirements for secure enterprise AI.

Why this matters in the SAP context

This shift is especially relevant in SAP landscapes because enterprise AI does not operate in isolation. It increasingly sits close to business-critical processes, sensitive enterprise data, and transactional systems. This makes governance of model access, prompt construction, downstream actions, and identity context far more important than in conventional AI experimentation.

As SAP customers adopt SAP Business AI, Joule, SAP AI Core, and broader AI-driven orchestration across the Business Suite, the discussion must move beyond classical infrastructure protection. The question is no longer only whether the platform is secure. The question is whether the AI-driven decision and action layer is governed with the right guardrails in place.

In practical terms, that means security must cover not only infrastructure, identity, APIs, and data, but also:

  • what the agent is allowed to see
  • what instructions it is allowed to follow
  • which tools and business actions it is permitted to invoke
  • when human approval is required
  • how actions are logged, reviewed, and constrained

The role of layered guardrails

A credible agentic AI security model cannot rely on one control alone. It must be layered.

For example:

  • Input filtering can help detect prompt injection before it reaches the model.
  • Data masking can reduce exposure of sensitive information in prompts, retrieved context, and tool outputs.
  • Orchestration policy can restrict which tools an agent can call and under what conditions.
  • Runtime isolation can help preserve customer separation and prevent unintended data leakage.
  • Human checkpoints can act as control gates for high-risk or irreversible actions.
  • Output filtering and audit logging can reduce unsafe responses and improve forensic visibility.

That is the core principle: no single guardrail is enough on its own. A content filter cannot enforce permissions. A permission model cannot detect prompt injection. A log can record an event, but it cannot stop it. Agentic AI security becomes credible only when these layers work together.

Why blast radius becomes a board-level issue

The real issue is not simply that AI agents are smarter interfaces. The issue is that they can become operational actors across enterprise systems.

A traditional application compromise may affect one workload, one database, or one business process. A poorly governed or compromised AI agent can potentially act across every system, workflow, and dataset it has been allowed to orchestrate.

That changes the blast radius dramatically.

This is why the security conversation must move beyond infrastructure hardening. Agentic AI security is about constraining autonomy, validating intent, limiting blast radius, and ensuring that sensitive actions do not happen without the right controls. The more capable the agent becomes, the more important these boundaries become.

Traditional cloud security vs. agentic AI security

 

Aspect

Traditional Cloud Security

Agentic AI Security

Primary focus

Securing infrastructure, applications, identities, and data

Securing the agent, its decisions, actions, and connected environment

Application behaviour

Fixed and developer-defined

Dynamic and runtime-driven

Main risk

Misconfiguration, unauthorized access, software vulnerabilities

Prompt injection, unsafe autonomy, tool misuse, decision manipulation

Attack surface

Networks, APIs, endpoints, code

Networks, APIs, code, prompts, context, tools, and agent workflows

Access model

Who can access what

Who can access what, what the agent is told, and what it can do next

Core controls

IAM, firewall, WAF, DLP, monitoring, encryption

Traditional controls plus guardrails, orchestration policy, isolation, auditability, and human approval

Blast radius

Often limited to a system or application boundary

Can extend across all systems the agent can orchestrate

Governance need

Access and configuration governance

Access, behaviour, decision, action, and data-flow governance

From concept to control: how SAP helps reduce blast radius in agentic AI

The security challenge in agentic AI is not only that an agent can generate responses. It is that the agent can increasingly orchestrate actions across enterprise systems, business processes, and sensitive data flows. That is where blast radius expands.

This is where SAP’s architecture becomes important.

With SAP AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, SAP provides a governed way to access foundation models. SAP documents the Generative AI Hub as a capability of SAP AI Core and SAP AI Launchpad that enables access to and orchestration of generative AI models within a governed environment. SAP also documents a Harmonized API that lets teams use different foundation models without changing client code. This matters because enterprise AI access should not depend on fragmented, model-specific integration patterns spread across the landscape. A governed model-access layer improves consistency, control, and policy enforcement.

On top of that, the Orchestration capability adds higher-level control points around the interaction itself. SAP documents orchestration as supporting modules such as templating, large language models, data masking, and content filtering, and describes the flow as including template rendering, text generation, and optional input/output transformations such as data masking or filtering. In practical terms, that makes orchestration highly relevant for input filtering, output filtering, prompt templating, and data masking in agentic AI scenarios.

This matters because security in agentic AI is no longer only about who can call an API. It is also about what context is passed to the model, what sensitive data must be masked, what unsafe instructions must be filtered, how prompts are structured, and how much freedom the agent should have in composing actions. In SAP terms, the combination of governed model access plus orchestration controls helps move the design away from ad hoc prompt handling and toward bounded, inspectable interaction patterns.

At the application layer, CAP and SAP BTP connectivity patterns are equally relevant. SAP documents principal propagation as the forwarding of a cloud user’s identity to a remote system. That matters because in agentic AI scenarios, downstream actions should ideally execute in the right user context instead of collapsing into a broad technical user. Preserving identity context helps maintain authorization boundaries, traceability, and accountability when an agent invokes connected systems. In simple terms, principal propagation helps reduce the risk that an AI-driven workflow quietly expands privilege across the landscape.

The architectural point is simple: in agentic AI, security must span multiple layers at once.

  • Generative AI Hub provides governed access to models.
  • Harmonized API helps standardize model consumption across providers.
  • Orchestration adds prompt templating, input/output filtering, and data masking controls around the interaction flow.
  • CAP and principal propagation help preserve user context and authorization boundaries in downstream actions.

That is how the discussion moves from generic AI guardrails to real enterprise architecture.

Traditional cloud security still protects infrastructure, networks, APIs, and workloads. But in agentic AI, SAP customers also need controls around how prompts are formed, how model access is governed, how sensitive data is masked, how outputs are filtered, and whose authority an agent is using when it acts.

That is how blast radius is reduced in agentic AI: not by trusting the agent more, but by constraining it through architecture.

Conclusion

Traditional cloud security remains essential, but it does not fully address the security model of agentic AI.

As AI agents begin to interpret instructions, invoke tools, and act across enterprise systems, security must extend beyond infrastructure and access control into filtering, masking, prompt governance, orchestration policy, identity propagation, and action boundaries. In SAP landscapes, that is where capabilities such as SAP AI Core, the Generative AI Hub, Harmonized API, Orchestration, CAP, and principal propagation become strategically important. Together, they help customers move from simply connecting AI to enterprise systems toward governing it safely.

The future of enterprise AI will not be defined only by how powerful agents become, but by how well their autonomy is bounded.

entry.title: From ‘What Is’ to ‘What If’ : Building a Dual-Model Payroll Impact Simulator for SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
In the SAP ecosystem, we often talk about "Meeting the customer where they are." When I started developing the Payroll Impact Simulator as a personal pilot project, I wanted to solve a universal pain point: the "black box" of payroll. Employees constantly ask how life changes—like a new baby or a 401k adjustment—will impact their take-home pay.

To solve this, I’ve developed a solution using a SAP Fiori frontend that communicates with Backend ECP OData APIs to trigger real-time simulations.

One Solution, Two Deployment Flavors

A key part of this pilot was ensuring flexibility. Depending on an organization’s landscape, the simulator can be deployed in two ways:

  1. SAP BTP Edition: The Fiori frontend is hosted on SAP BTP, providing a clean, side-by-side extension that leverages BTP's robust integration capabilities.

  2. ECP-Embedded Edition: For customers without a BTP footprint, the app can be hosted directly within Employee Central Payroll (ECP). This version is called directly from SuccessFactors using EC-ECP Single Sign-On (SSO) for a frictionless user experience.


The User Journey: From "What Is" to "What If"

The app is designed as an Employee Self-Service (ESS) Add-on that empowers users to visualize their financial future without risk. Even Payroll Admins can simulate the Payrolls for the authorized pay group population. 

  • Scenario Selection: Employees choose from various "What-If" triggers, such as office location changes, salary adjustments, or tax withholding updates.

  • Real-Time Simulation: The app triggers the SAP ECP payroll driver for the user’s specific country to calculate the result.

  • Instant Comparison: Users receive a side-by-side visualized comparison of their current "as-is" payslip versus the "to-be" simulated scenario.

  • Localization: For Every Country the UI can be configured based upon which infotypes need to be allowed for simulation. 

 

Why This Matters for HR Teams

Beyond the "cool factor" of real-time modeling, this pilot addresses three critical business challenges:

  • Transparency & Trust: Employees gain a clear "payroll story" and explore changes with confidence.

  • Reduced Administrative Load: By providing a self-service tool, HR and Payroll teams see a significant drop in routine "what-if" inquiries.

  • Data Privacy: A core design principle is that the data is transient. No simulation data is stored, ensuring 100% compliance and security.

 YouTube Link - Demo Video

 

Conclusion

This pilot demonstrates how SAP Fiori and OData APIs can be used to extend the standard SuccessFactors experience. Whether hosted on BTP or directly in ECP, the goal remains the same: taking the guesswork out of payroll.

 

I’d love to hear from the community—how are you handling "what-if" payroll scenarios today? Would a dual-deployment model like this fit your current landscape?

entry.title: Re: BAPI_PO_CREATE1 PO not selectable in MIGO – missing C...
entry.content:
Why ME21N works but BAPI does not is because, ME21N triggers the full pricing procedure automatically, pulling in PB00/PBXX and other conditions. BAPI_PO_CREATE1 does not run pricing by itself. It only posts what you explicitly pass in the structures. If you don’t populate the condition tables correctly, the PO will be created without conditions. Even if you fill POCOND, the system ignores them unless the pricing flag is set and the structures are consistent with the item data.

Commonly missed requirements

1) Pricing Flag
In the BAPI import parameters, you must set PRICING = 'X'. Without this, SAP won’t run the pricing procedure, even if conditions are passed.

2) Condition Step/Counter
Fields like COND_ST_NO (step number) and COND_COUNT (counter) must be filled correctly. If they are missing, SAP discards the condition lines silently.

3) Change ID
Must be 'I' (Insert) for new conditions. If left blank, the system ignores the entry.

4) Consistency with Item Data
NET_PRICE, PRICE_UNIT, COND_VALUE, COND_UNIT, and CURRENCY must align with the PO item. If there’s a mismatch, the condition is dropped.

5) Header vs. Item Conditions
If you only populate header conditions but not item conditions, MIGO won’t see the item as valuated. At least one item‑level condition (PB00/PBXX) is required.

entry.title: Re: How to add a check for requisitioner for purchase req...
entry.content:
You are right that the WORKORDER_UPDATE BAdI does not give you the full detail of operations/components at the right point in time. For your requirement, validating requisitioner before PR creation from a PM order, you need an enhancement spot that has access to both the order data and the PR creation logic before commit.

Explore with exits like IWO10009 (general order save check) or IWO10018 (specific PR creation check). Both have access to operations/components and allow you to raise an error that blocks saving/PR creation but keeps the user in the order transaction.

entry.title: Re: PO not display in ME28 when using recorded script
entry.content:
This is a classic case of PO release strategy behavior rather than a macro/VBA recording issue. The reason your POs appear in ME28 but your colleagues’ don’t is almost certainly due to release strategy conditions not being met in their created POs.

My advice is to take one PO created manually and one created via macro, run report ME2N or check table EKKO, and compare the key fields used in release strategy classification. You will immediately see which field is missing or different.

entry.title: Clean Core – Need Released API Alternatives for Standard Tables and Classes in S/4HANA 2023 (On-Prem
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

We are currently working on Clean Core / ABAP Cloud compliance for our custom ABAP developments in S/4HANA 2023 (On-Premise).

As part of this effort, we are replacing direct usage of non-released objects (standard tables/classes) with released APIs (CDS views / RAP / whitelisted objects) in line with SAP’s Clean Core principles.

Objects Identified in Our Custom Code

We need guidance in identifying released alternatives (APIs / CDS Views / RAP BOs) for the following objects:

Classes

  • CL_EAML_AUSP_LFE_DATA_HANDLER

Standard Tables

  • ILOA
  • ADRC
  • ADRV

CDS Views (Need confirmation on release status or Alternative)

  • I_EQUIPMENTDATA
  • I_LINEARASSETMANAGEMENTDATA

Our Questions

  1. Released API Availability
    Are there any released CDS views / RAP BOs / APIs available as alternatives for:
    • Functional Location / Equipment-related data (ILOA)
    • Address data (ADRC, ADRV)
    • Class CL_EAML_AUSP_LFE_DATA_HANDLER
  2. CDS View Release Status
    Are the following CDS views officially released for ABAP Cloud / Clean Core usage?
    • I_EQUIPMENTDATA
    • I_LINEARASSETMANAGEMENTDATA
  3. Best Practice in Absence of APIs
    In scenarios where no released API is available in S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise, what is the recommended approach:
    • Create a wrapper class and expose as released API?
    • Build custom CDS views on top of tables?
    • Use key-user extensibility or side-by-side extensions?

What We Have Done So Far

  • Executed ATC checks (ABAP_CLOUD_READINESS / Clean Core checks)
  • Identified usage of non-released objects
  • Started replacing with released CDS views where available, but facing gaps for the above objects

Objective

Our goal is to:

  • Ensure compliance with Clean Core principles
  • Use only released and upgrade-safe artifacts
  • Avoid direct dependency on standard tables and unreleased classes

Request

If anyone has:

  • Mapping references (Standard Table → Released CDS/API)
  • SAP Notes or documentation links
  • Practical experience handling these objects in S/4HANA 2023 On-Prem

your guidance would be highly appreciated.

entry.title: Clean Core – Alternative to Implicit Enhancement in Program SAPFC68A for Work Center Save Event
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

We are currently working on Clean Core compliance for our custom developments in S/4HANA 2023 (On-Premise) and have encountered a challenge regarding enhancements.

Current Scenario

We have implemented an implicit enhancement in standard SAP program SAPFC68A to capture data when Work Center master data is saved.

This enhancement is currently used to trigger custom logic during the save operation.

Clean Core Concern

As per Clean Core guidelines, implicit enhancements are not recommended, since they:

  • Modify SAP standard behavior
  • Are not upgrade-safe
  • Violate extensibility principles

What We Have Checked

We explored standard extensibility options but could not find a suitable alternative:

  • BAdIs (Business Add-Ins)
  • BTEs (Business Transaction Events)
  • User Exits

Unfortunately, none of the available options seem to support triggering logic specifically during Work Center save.

Our Questions

  1. Recommended Approach
    What is the SAP-recommended approach in Clean Core for handling such scenarios where:
    • We need to trigger custom logic during Work Center save
    • No suitable BAdI/User Exit is available

  2. Alternative Extensibility Options
    Are there any:

    • Released BAdIs (even indirect ones)
    • RAP-based or event-based mechanisms
    • Business Object events or Enterprise Event Enablement options

    that can be used for Work Center updates?

  3. Fallback Strategy
    In absence of any released enhancement option:
    • Is implicit enhancement acceptable as a temporary workaround?

Objective

Our goal is to:

  • Remove implicit enhancements
  • Follow upgrade-safe and compliant extensibility patterns
  • Align fully with Clean Core principles

Request

If anyone has:

  • Experience handling similar Work Center enhancement scenarios
  • Knowledge of hidden/released BAdIs or events
  • SAP Notes or recommended architecture

your guidance would be highly appreciated.

entry.title: Clean Core – Released API Alternative for Function Module CRAP_WORKCENTER_CREATE in S/4HANA 2023
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

We are currently working on Clean Core / ABAP Cloud compliance in S/4HANA 2023 (On-Premise) and are reviewing our custom developments to replace usage of non-released function modules.

Current Scenario

In our custom implementation, we are using the function module CRAP_WORKCENTER_CREATE to create Work Center master data programmatically.

However, this function module is not released for use under Clean Core / ABAP Cloud guidelines and is flagged during ATC checks.

Our Challenge

We need to identify a released and upgrade-safe alternative to achieve the same functionality (i.e., Work Center creation).

What We Have Checked

  • Searched for released BAPIs  for Work Center creation

Our Questions

  1. Released API Availability
    Is there any released  BAPI / RAP BO available for:
    • Creating Work Center master data
    • Updating Work Center data
  2. Recommended Approach
    What is the SAP-recommended approach in Clean Core when:
    • A required standard function module is not released
    • No direct API is available

Objective

Our goal is to:

  • Replace usage of non-released function modules
  • Ensure upgrade-safe and compliant development
  • Align fully with Clean Core principles

Request

If anyone has:

  • Knowledge of released APIs for Work Center create/update
  • SAP Notes or official documentation references
  • Experience handling similar scenarios

your guidance would be highly appreciated.

entry.title: Managing Multiple Employments in SAP SuccessFactors Learning: Person-Centric Experience
entry.content:
When Learning is employment based, each new employment for an existing employee means a new user is created in Learning, training can be assigned. Administrators then need to merge the records to maintain a unique profile per employee with all the training assignments and history.  We have already enhanced the user experience so that employees can view all assignments, pending approvals and surveys in a single view, however they may be requested to switch employment to take certain actions (like launching a course).

When Learning becomes Person Based, each new employment created in Employee Central will generate a new employment record in Learning aggregated against the same user profile. Removing the burden of continuously merging internal employee's users, ensuring continuity in learning throughout their employments.

An employee will access Learning to view AND act on ALL their assignments, pending approvals and training completion for all their employments without the need to sign-in with different accounts.

Adopting the Person Centric Experience in Learning entails:

  1. Preparing your user data (select a single user profile per employees, merge other records to the main user)
  2. Set-up the new Person Connector (replacing the SF User connector)
  3. Migrate the tenant

Please note the following prerequisites:

  1. You must have more than one preview environment
    This is because to enable Person Based Learning, you need to migrate the instance and this will change how the user data is handled in the application. Therefore this system will behave differently than you production instance. It is important to keep a non-production instance that is NOT person based to match your production system where you can carry out tests and change management.
  2. Employee Central enabled
  3. Your Platform application is migrated to IAS enabled
  4. The PLT–LMS integration of the preview instance is sealed 

If you are interested and meet the prerequisites, you can submit a support ticket after the 1H2026 preview release (please share your system details and evidence  that the above conditions are met) so we that can enable a feature toggle in your instance to enable the migration feature.

To understand what the migration entails and how the application behaves when it is person based, please read the materials available here , the content provided are as follow:

  • Transition Guide = covers the overall migration process and end user experience post migration.
  • Migration Guide = covers details of the migration process
  • Person Connector Guide = covers implementation details for the new connector
  • Person Connector Integration Center: File Definition = Integration Center icd template
  • Person Connector Integration Center: File Specification = Integration Center field mapping details
  • Person Connector Fields and Properties = Person connector mapping and properties

entry.title: Get the SAP Enterprise Support Guidance You Need in SAP for Me
entry.content:
SAP for Me now includes a new feature that delivers personalized enablement recommendations based on your purchased products, bringing SAP Enterprise Support value maps knowledge base resources into one place so it’s easier to find the guidance and supporting materials you need. 

Customers rely on SAP Enterprise Support value maps for structured guidance and enablement. This experience now comes together in a more intuitive journey that makes discovery seamless and personalization more precise. With a simplified, centralized, and context-aware approach, guidance surfaces at the right time to support confident action. 

SAP for Me provides direct access to SAP Enterprise Support value maps knowledge base resources for all purchased products, consolidating key enablement materials into one space aligned with SAP’s post-sales standards for structure, clarity, and ease of use. 

Benefits 

Improved Discoverability 

SAP for Me brings SAP Enterprise Support resources together, simplifying how you find and use relevant guidance. 

Get the Guidance You Need, When You Need It 

Guidance is now surfaced directly within SAP for Me, giving you immediate access to expert-curated resources that support enablement and informed decision-making. 

In SAP Enterprise Support value maps, a knowledge base brings together helpful information and resources in a structured way, including: 

  • Commonly searched topics 
  • Step-by-step guidance 
  • Release readiness materials 
  • Knowledge accelerators 
  • Implementation guidance 
  • Troubleshooting support 
  • Value packages 
  • Scenario-based articles 

Each article is designed for quick understanding and practical application. 

Knowledge Base Articles on Purchased Product Detail Pages 

Within SAP for Me, the Portfolio & Products dashboard now includes an enhanced Knowledge & Learning tab for each purchased product. This space includes: 

  • Expert-curated articles 
  • Cross-solution scenario guidance 
  • Step-by-step implementation and operational tips 
  • Security and optimization practices 
  • Direct links to relevant SAP assets 

More than 150 pages of structured guidance now span 40+ SAP products, providing actionable recommendations to support you at every stage. 
 

The updated experience keeps essential SAP Enterprise Support value map resources close at hand, supporting smoother onboarding, enablement, and continued success with your SAP solutions. 
 
Take the next step with SAP Enterprise Support value maps on SAP Community. Connect with experts, explore additional resources, and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals. The SAP Foundational Success Plan is part of SAP’s Services and Support portfolio and is included with every cloud solution. It provides onboarding, cloud operations, and preventive support at no extra cost, including SAP Enterprise Support. Value Maps are a key part of this offering.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Migration Assessment Agent (Part 2 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 2 OF 6

SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Migration Assessment Agent

From a single ReAct agent to a full multi-tool LangGraph state machine with human-in-the-loop — assessing custom code health, table complexity, configuration footprint, and integration dependencies to produce a scored S/4HANA migration readiness report.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 2 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 2 builds the SAP Migration Assessment Agent in two stages. Stage A extends the Part 1 ReAct pattern into a single agent with human-in-the-loop that assesses all four migration dimensions — custom code, table complexity, configuration footprint, and integration dependencies. Stage B evolves this into a LangGraph state machine with conditional routing, where the agent's path through the assessment changes based on what it finds. Both stages produce a scored migration readiness report with dimension-level findings.

Series Context

This is Part 2 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice. Part 1 established the foundational concepts — agent vs skill vs pipeline, the ReAct pattern, LangGraph architecture, and a first working agent for SAP data quality assessment. If you haven't read Part 1, the agent state schema and tool definitions introduced there are the starting point for everything built here.

Part 2 applies those foundations to a real SAP migration use case — assessing an SAP landscape for S/4HANA readiness across four dimensions. This is the kind of assessment that currently takes SAP migration teams weeks of manual analysis. The agent does it autonomously, with a human-in-the-loop checkpoint before the final report is produced.

WHY MIGRATION ASSESSMENT IS AN IDEAL AGENT USE CASE 

Why Migration Assessment Fits the Agent Pattern

S/4HANA migration assessment is one of the most time-consuming and inconsistently executed activities in SAP consulting. Two experienced consultants assessing the same landscape often produce different findings because the process is manual, undocumented, and dependent on tribal knowledge about what to look for.

It fits the agent pattern for three reasons:

  • The path is not predetermined — what the agent looks for in custom code depends on how much custom code exists. What it investigates in integrations depends on which adapters it finds. The assessment adapts to the landscape.
  • It requires multi-tool reasoning — no single query answers the readiness question. The agent must combine findings across custom code, data volume, configuration, and interfaces to produce a meaningful score.
  • Human review before action is natural — a migration readiness report should be reviewed by a consultant before being shared with a client. Human-in-the-loop isn't a constraint here, it's the correct workflow.

Assessment Dimension

What It Signals for Migration

Custom code health

Volume of Z-objects, modifications, and deprecated API usage — signals clean core compliance gap

Table complexity

Record volumes and growth rates across key business objects — signals data migration effort

Configuration footprint

Scope of customizing across modules — signals business process complexity and testing effort

Integration dependencies

Interface count, adapter types, and external system connections — signals technical migration risk

STAGE A: SINGLE AGENT WITH HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP 

Stage A — Extending the ReAct Pattern with Human-in-the-Loop

Stage A extends the Part 1 ReAct agent into a migration assessment agent. The core pattern is the same — the agent reasons and acts in a loop using SAP-specific tools. Two things are added: all four assessment dimensions as tools, and a human-in-the-loop checkpoint before the final report is generated.

Goal: assess landscape → Custom code check → Table complexity → Config footprint → Integration scan → Human review → Scored report
Assessment State Schema

Extend the SAPAssessmentState from Part 1 with migration-specific fields:

# migration_agent.py — extends Part 1 state schema
from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional, Dict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
 
class MigrationAssessmentState(TypedDict):
    goal:                str
    system_id:           str          # SAP system being assessed
    # Dimension findings
    custom_code:         Optional[dict]  # Z-objects, mods, deprecated APIs
    table_complexity:    Optional[dict]  # Volumes and growth rates
    config_footprint:    Optional[dict]  # Customizing scope per module
    integration_deps:    Optional[dict]  # Interface count and types
    # Scoring
    dimension_scores:    Dict[str, int]   # Score per dimension 0-100
    overall_score:       Optional[int]    # Weighted overall readiness score
    readiness_level:     Optional[str]    # HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW / CRITICAL
    # Control
    findings:            List[dict]
    human_review_needed: bool
    human_approved:      bool
    final_report:        Optional[str]
Migration Assessment Tools
# tools/migration_tools.py
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb, json, os
 
def get_conn():
    return hdb.connect(address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'], port=443,
                       user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
                       password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'], encrypt=True)
 
@tool
def assess_custom_code(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess custom code health — count Z-objects, modifications,
    and usage of deprecated APIs that block S/4HANA migration."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Count Z-programs and includes
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT COUNT(*) as z_program_count
        FROM TADIR
        WHERE DEVCLASS LIKE 'Z%' AND OBJECT IN ('PROG', 'FUGR', 'CLAS')
    ''')
    z_count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
 
    # Count SAP standard modifications (risky for migration)
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT COUNT(*) as mod_count
        FROM TADIR
        WHERE DEVCLASS NOT LIKE 'Z%'
        AND DEVCLASS NOT LIKE '$%'
        AND DEVCLASS != 'LOCAL'
        AND OBJECT IN ('PROG', 'FUGR', 'CLAS', 'DOMA', 'DTEL')
    ''')
    mod_count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
 
    return json.dumps({
        'z_program_count': z_count,
        'modification_count': mod_count,
        'clean_core_risk': 'HIGH' if mod_count > 100 else
                           'MEDIUM' if mod_count > 20 else 'LOW'
    })
 
@tool
def assess_table_complexity(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess data volume complexity across key business object tables."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Key tables that drive migration effort
    key_tables = ['BKPF', 'BSEG', 'VBAK', 'VBAP', 'EKKO',
                  'EKPO', 'MARA', 'KNA1', 'LFA1', 'AUFK']
    volumes = {}
 
    for table in key_tables:
        try:
            cursor.execute(f'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table}')
            volumes[table] = cursor.fetchone()[0]
        except Exception:
            volumes[table] = 'unavailable'
 
    total_records = sum(v for v in volumes.values() if isinstance(v, int))
    return json.dumps({
        'table_volumes': volumes,
        'total_key_records': total_records,
        'volume_risk': 'HIGH' if total_records > 500_000_000 else
                       'MEDIUM' if total_records > 50_000_000 else 'LOW'
    })
 
@tool
def assess_config_footprint(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess configuration and customizing footprint across SAP modules."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Count customizing entries per application area
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT LEFT(TABNAME, 2) as module_prefix,
               COUNT(*) as custom_entry_count
        FROM DD02L
        WHERE TABCLASS = 'C'
        GROUP BY LEFT(TABNAME, 2)
        ORDER BY custom_entry_count DESC
        LIMIT 10
    ''')
    modules = {row[0]: row[1] for row in cursor.fetchall()}
    total_custom = sum(modules.values())
 
    return json.dumps({
        'customizing_by_module': modules,
        'total_customizing_entries': total_custom,
        'config_complexity': 'HIGH' if total_custom > 10000 else
                             'MEDIUM' if total_custom > 2000 else 'LOW'
    })
 
@tool
def assess_integration_dependencies(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess interface and integration dependency footprint."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Count RFC destinations (external system connections)
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT RFCTYPE, COUNT(*) as connection_count
        FROM RFCDES
        WHERE RFCTYPE != 'L'
        -- Exclude loopback connections
        GROUP BY RFCTYPE
    ''')
    rfc_connections = {row[0]: row[1] for row in cursor.fetchall()}
    total_rfc = sum(rfc_connections.values())
 
    return json.dumps({
        'rfc_connections_by_type': rfc_connections,
        'total_external_connections': total_rfc,
        'integration_risk': 'HIGH' if total_rfc > 50 else
                            'MEDIUM' if total_rfc > 15 else 'LOW'
    })
Stage A Agent — ReAct with Human-in-the-Loop
# Stage A: Single ReAct agent with human-in-the-loop checkpoint
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from tools.migration_tools import (assess_custom_code,
    assess_table_complexity, assess_config_footprint,
    assess_integration_dependencies)
 
# MemorySaver enables human-in-the-loop by persisting state
memory = MemorySaver()
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
tools = [
    assess_custom_code,
    assess_table_complexity,
    assess_config_footprint,
    assess_integration_dependencies
]
 
system_prompt = '''You are an SAP migration assessment agent.
Assess the S/4HANA migration readiness of the SAP system provided.

Follow this sequence:
1. Assess custom code health
2. Assess table complexity and data volumes
3. Assess configuration and customizing footprint
4. Assess integration and interface dependencies
5. Score each dimension 0-100 (100 = fully ready, 0 = critical blocker)
6. Calculate a weighted overall readiness score
7. Present findings summary and WAIT for human review before final report

Scoring weights:
  Custom code: 30%  |  Table complexity: 25%
  Configuration: 20%  |  Integrations: 25%

Readiness levels:
  80-100: HIGH — proceed with migration planning
  60-79:  MEDIUM — address findings before committing timeline
  40-59:  LOW — significant remediation required
  0-39:   CRITICAL — do not proceed without major remediation'''
 
# interrupt_before=['tools'] pauses agent for human review
# before it calls the final report generation tool
agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=tools,
    checkpointer=memory,
    state_modifier=system_prompt
)

Running Stage A with Human Review

# Run the agent — it pauses for human review after dimension assessment
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
 
# Thread ID maintains state across the pause/resume cycle
config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': 'migration-assessment-001'}}
 
# Phase 1: Agent assesses all four dimensions
result = agent.invoke({
    'messages': [HumanMessage(content='''
        Assess the S/4HANA migration readiness of system PRD.
        Assess all four dimensions and present a summary for review.
    ''')]
}, config=config)
 
# Agent presents findings and waits — human reviews here
print('--- AGENT FINDINGS FOR REVIEW ---')
print(result['messages'][-1].content)
 
# Phase 2: Human approves and agent generates final scored report
human_input = input('Review complete. Approve report generation? (yes/no): ')
 
if human_input.lower() == 'yes':
    final = agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content='''
            Findings approved. Generate the full scored migration
            readiness report with dimension scores, overall score,
            readiness level, and prioritized recommendations.
        ''')]
    }, config=config)
    print(final['messages'][-1].content)

The thread_id in config is what makes human-in-the-loop work. LangGraph persists the agent's state — all findings, tool results, and reasoning — against this ID. When the agent resumes after human approval, it picks up exactly where it left off with full context intact.

STAGE B: MULTI-TOOL AGENT WITH CONDITIONAL ROUTING 

Stage B — LangGraph State Machine with Conditional Routing

Stage A uses a single ReAct agent that assesses all dimensions sequentially. Stage B evolves this into a LangGraph state graph where each dimension is a dedicated node, and the agent routes conditionally based on findings. If custom code risk is CRITICAL, the agent flags for immediate escalation rather than continuing to assessment dimensions that won't change the outcome.

START → Custom code node → Route: CRITICAL? → Escalate / Continue → Table + Config + Integration nodes → Score node → Human checkpoint → Report node
Why Conditional Routing Matters for Migration Assessment

A sequential agent always runs all four dimensions regardless of what it finds. A graph-based agent with conditional routing can:

  • Skip downstream checks when a critical blocker makes the overall score irrelevant
  • Run high-risk dimension checks first and escalate early
  • Take different reporting paths based on overall readiness level
  • Pause at different points depending on finding severity

This is the difference between an agent that executes a fixed workflow faster and an agent that genuinely adapts its approach based on what it discovers.

Stage B State Graph Definition

# Stage B: LangGraph state machine with conditional routing
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
import json
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
memory = MemorySaver()
 
# Node: Assess custom code dimension
def assess_custom_code_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    result = json.loads(assess_custom_code.invoke(
        {'system_id': state['system_id']}
    ))
    score = 100 - min(100, result['modification_count'] // 2)
    state['custom_code'] = result
    state['dimension_scores']['custom_code'] = score
 
    # Flag critical blocker if modifications exceed threshold
    if result['modification_count'] > 500:
        state['findings'].append({
            'dimension': 'custom_code',
            'severity': 'CRITICAL',
            'message': f"{result['modification_count']} SAP standard modifications detected — clean core remediation required before migration"
        })
        state['human_review_needed'] = True
    return state
 
# Node: Assess table complexity
def assess_table_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    result = json.loads(assess_table_complexity.invoke(
        {'system_id': state['system_id']}
    ))
    score = 100 - (20 if result['volume_risk'] == 'HIGH' else
                   10 if result['volume_risk'] == 'MEDIUM' else 0)
    state['table_complexity'] = result
    state['dimension_scores']['table_complexity'] = score
    return state
 
# Node: Assess configuration footprint
def assess_config_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    result = json.loads(assess_config_footprint.invoke(
        {'system_id': state['system_id']}
    ))
    score = 100 - (30 if result['config_complexity'] == 'HIGH' else
                   15 if result['config_complexity'] == 'MEDIUM' else 0)
    state['config_footprint'] = result
    state['dimension_scores']['config_footprint'] = score
    return state
 
# Node: Assess integration dependencies
def assess_integration_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    result = json.loads(assess_integration_dependencies.invoke(
        {'system_id': state['system_id']}
    ))
    score = 100 - (35 if result['integration_risk'] == 'HIGH' else
                   15 if result['integration_risk'] == 'MEDIUM' else 0)
    state['integration_deps'] = result
    state['dimension_scores']['integration_deps'] = score
    return state
 
# Node: Calculate overall score and readiness level
def score_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    scores = state['dimension_scores']
    weighted = (
        scores.get('custom_code', 0)      * 0.30 +
        scores.get('table_complexity', 0) * 0.25 +
        scores.get('config_footprint', 0) * 0.20 +
        scores.get('integration_deps', 0) * 0.25
    )
    state['overall_score'] = round(weighted)
    state['readiness_level'] = (
        'HIGH'     if weighted >= 80 else
        'MEDIUM'   if weighted >= 60 else
        'LOW'      if weighted >= 40 else 'CRITICAL'
    )
    state['human_review_needed'] = True
    return state
 
# Node: Generate final scored report using LLM
def report_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
    response = llm.invoke([
        SystemMessage(content='''Generate a structured SAP migration
        readiness report. Include: Executive Summary with overall score,
        dimension-level findings with scores, critical blockers,
        prioritized recommendations with effort estimates.
        Be specific and reference actual findings.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Generate report from state: {json.dumps({k: v for k, v in state.items() if k != "messages"}, default=str)}')
    ])
    state['final_report'] = response.content
    return state
 
# Conditional routing — escalate immediately if critical blocker found
def route_after_custom_code(state: MigrationAssessmentState) -> str:
    critical = any(f['severity'] == 'CRITICAL' for f in state['findings'])
    return 'escalate' if critical else 'continue_assessment'
 
# Build the state graph
graph = StateGraph(MigrationAssessmentState)
 
# Add nodes
graph.add_node('assess_custom_code', assess_custom_code_node)
graph.add_node('assess_table', assess_table_node)
graph.add_node('assess_config', assess_config_node)
graph.add_node('assess_integration', assess_integration_node)
graph.add_node('score', score_node)
graph.add_node('report', report_node)
 
# Set entry point
graph.set_entry_point('assess_custom_code')
 
# Conditional edge after custom code — escalate or continue
graph.add_conditional_edges(
    'assess_custom_code',
    route_after_custom_code,
    {
        'escalate':            END,
        # CRITICAL: stop and flag for human intervention
        'continue_assessment': 'assess_table'
    }
)
 
# Sequential edges for remaining dimensions
graph.add_edge('assess_table',       'assess_config')
graph.add_edge('assess_config',      'assess_integration')
graph.add_edge('assess_integration', 'score')
 
# Human-in-the-loop: interrupt before report generation
graph.add_edge('score', 'report')
graph.add_edge('report', END)
 
# Compile with checkpointer for human-in-the-loop support
app = graph.compile(
    checkpointer=memory,
    interrupt_before=['report']
    # Pause before report — human reviews score first
)

Running Stage B

# Run Stage B — agent routes conditionally based on findings
initial_state = MigrationAssessmentState(
    goal='Assess S/4HANA migration readiness',
    system_id='PRD',
    custom_code=None, table_complexity=None,
    config_footprint=None, integration_deps=None,
    dimension_scores={}, overall_score=None,
    readiness_level=None, findings=[],
    human_review_needed=False, human_approved=False,
    final_report=None
)
 
config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': 'migration-stageb-001'}}
 
# Phase 1: Agent assesses all dimensions and scores
result = app.invoke(initial_state, config=config)
 
# Check if critical blocker caused early escalation
if result['findings'] and result['final_report'] is None:
    critical = [f for f in result['findings'] if f['severity'] == 'CRITICAL']
    if critical:
        print('CRITICAL BLOCKER DETECTED — escalation required')
        print(json.dumps(critical, indent=2))
else:
    # Human reviews score before report is generated
    print(f"Overall Score: {result['overall_score']}/100"
          f" — Readiness: {result['readiness_level']}")
    print(f"Dimension Scores: {result['dimension_scores']}")
 
    approval = input('Approve final report generation? (yes/no): ')
    if approval.lower() == 'yes':
        result['human_approved'] = True
        final = app.invoke(result, config=config)
        print(final['final_report'])

interrupt_before=['report'] tells LangGraph to pause execution before the report node runs. The agent has completed all assessment nodes and scoring — the state holds all findings and dimension scores — but the final report hasn't been generated yet. This is the correct human-in-the-loop point: the human sees scores and findings, approves, then the report is generated with their oversight recorded in the audit trail.

STAGE A VS STAGE B — WHEN TO USE EACH 

Choosing Between Stage A and Stage B

 

Consideration

Stage A

Stage B

Simplicity

Stage A — single agent, less code, easier to debug

Stage B — explicit graph, more code but clearer execution path

Conditional routing

Stage A — model decides order implicitly

Stage B — routing is explicit and testable

Early escalation

Stage A — agent may complete all steps before flagging critical

Stage B — graph routes to END immediately on CRITICAL finding

Audit trail

Stage A — reasoning trace in message history

Stage B — explicit node execution path plus message history

Recommended for

Stage A — prototyping and smaller landscapes

Stage B — production deployments and complex landscapes

What's Next — Part 3

  • Part 3: SAP Exception Log Triage Agent — the agent monitors logs autonomously, identifies patterns across multiple error types, correlates related issues, and recommends remediation actions without human triage. Introduces the observer pattern for continuous agent monitoring.
  • Part 4: SAP Master Data Quality Agent — detects and prioritizes data quality issues across master data domains, generates a remediation plan with effort scoring, and integrates with the RAG pipeline from Series 1.
  • Part 5: S/4HANA Readiness Agent — extends Part 2 with clean core analysis, deprecated function module detection, and BAdI/enhancement spot mapping.
  • Part 6: Multi-Agent Orchestration — all four agents working together under an orchestrator that delegates assessment dimensions and synthesizes findings into a unified landscape report.

The migration assessment agent built in this article is the most complex single-agent implementation in this series. Parts 3–5 follow the same patterns — tool definitions, state schema, conditional routing, human-in-the-loop — applied to different SAP domains. If Stage B makes sense here, the remaining parts will feel familiar.

entry.title: Inconsistent Billed Revenue Display Across Event-Based Revenue Recognition Apps
entry.content:
In a SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system, we are observing inconsistencies in how billed revenue is displayed across different apps for customer projects.

Please find the observations and expectations below:

1. App: Event-Based Revenue Recognition – Projects

  • Available:
    The app displays:
    • Billed Revenue
    • Sales Deduction/Surcharge
  • Missing:
    A field showing the net invoiced amount (i.e., Billed Revenue adjusted by Sales Deduction/Surcharge)
  • Expectation:
    The net amount (after deductions/surcharges) would be the most relevant figure, as it reflects the actual invoiced value considered for revenue adjustment.

2. Period View (same app)

  • Observation:
    Only the total billed revenue is visible.
    Sales deductions/surcharges are not shown, nor is the adjusted (net) invoiced amount.

3. App: Revenue Recognition – Event-Based Projects

  • Same observation as point 1:
    Missing visibility of the net invoiced amount derived from billed revenue and deductions.

4. App: Project Profitability

  • Available:
    Only Billed Revenue is displayed
  • Missing:
    • Sales Deduction/Surcharge
    • Net invoiced amount (after adjustments)

5. App: Sales Volume – Flexible Analysis

  • Observation:
    This app displays the actual invoiced amount along with corresponding invoice references.
  • Note:
    This highlights the gap, as the invoiced (net) value is visible here but not consistently reflected in the other apps mentioned above.

Question

Is this standard system behavior in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, or is there a recommended way (e.g., configuration, CDS view extension, or custom analytics) to consistently display the missing information across these apps?

entry.title: SAP SD Rollout for New Company Code in Different Country
entry.content:
I am currently working on an SAP SD rollout for a client. We have successfully implemented SAP for their operations in Country X, and we are now expanding the solution to Country Y as a new branch with a separate Company Code.

The business processes in both countries are virtually identical (Standard Sales, Returns, Credit Memos, etc.).I know i have to create different sales org. For sure.I am seeking advice on the best architectural approach regarding the following:

1. Sales Document Types:

Since the business processes are the same, is it recommended to create new Sales Document Types (e.g., ZYOR for Country Y vs. ZXOR for Country X)? Or is it better practice to reuse the existing Document Types and simply assign them to the new Sales Areas? I am concerned about maintaining a clean configuration and whether separate Document Types are necessary for different Company Codes/Countries if the logic remains the same.

2. Distribution Channels:

Both countries operate using "Local" and "Export" sales. Should I create country-specific Distribution Channels (e.g., L1/E1 for Country X and L2/E2 for Country Y)? Or should I use common, unified Distribution Channels (e.g., 10 for Local and 20 for Export) across both Sales Organizations?

entry.title: Minhas impressões do SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte #SITBH2026
entry.content:
No dia 21 de março tive a oportunidade de participar de mais uma edição do SAP Inside Track, desta vez em Belo Horizonte. O evento aconteceu no Localiza Labs e, sinceramente, foi uma experiência incrível.

A estrutura chamou muita atenção. Foram 5 tracks acontecendo em paralelo, com conteúdos para todos os perfis, desde palestras e apresentações técnicas até treinamentos hands-on e estudos de caso. É aquele tipo de evento que você precisa até escolher bem onde ir, porque tudo parece interessante ao mesmo tempo.

A atmosfera estava realmente vibrante do começo ao fim. Foram quase 300 pessoas presentes, todas conectadas pelo objetivo de aprender, compartilhar e evoluir dentro do ecossistema SAP. É esse tipo de energia que faz o SAP Inside Track ser tão especial.

Para mim, teve também um lado muito importante que sempre valorizo nesses eventos, pois é o lugar onde tenho oportunidade de rever amigos da comunidade, trocar ideias, conhecer novas soluções e entender melhor o que os parceiros SAP estão trazendo de inovação no Brasil, especialmente em um polo tão forte como Belo Horizonte. E claro… não podemos esquecer dos brindes ... os sorteios estavam bem disputados, como sempre, e ajudam a deixar o evento ainda mais leve e divertido. Eu ganhei uma Capivara!

 

Outro ponto que me marcou foi ver o retorno do evento a Belo Horizonte. Desde 2019 não tínhamos uma edição por lá, e lembrar das edições de 2018 e 2019, todas muito bem-sucedidas, o que reforça o quanto a cidade sempre teve um papel importante na comunidade SAP.

Belo Horizonte, sem dúvida, continua sendo um dos grandes polos de tecnologia SAP no Brasil. Isso fica claro tanto pela qualidade das apresentações quanto pelo engajamento dos profissionais que participam.

E não posso deixar de destacar a organização, que foi um show à parte. Tudo muito bem planejado, com cuidado nos detalhes, garantindo conforto, uma estrutura excelente e, principalmente, um nível técnico alto e inovador.

E você, participou do evento? O que achou?

Compartilha aqui suas impressões e, se tiver, manda também algumas fotos desse momento incrível da comunidade!

#SITBH

@Marssel700

entry.title: Re: Minhas impressões do SAP Inside Track Belo Horizonte #SITBH2026

entry.title: How different LLMs answer the same innocent question
entry.content:
Before this 2026 April Fools week finishes, we wanted to submit an innocent question to several Ai agents to compare the way they reason and behave.

User prompt

Do you see anything special in these 3 phrases?

Phrase 1: We lie down, right?

Phrase 2: Our best loyal friend arrived.

Phrase 3: I do see some light.

 

Answer from GPT-5

Yes — each sentence hides an animal across word boundaries.

  • We LIE D(OW)N, right? → LION (L I from “lie,” O N from “down”)
  • BEst + ARRived → BEAR (BE + AR)
  • sEE + LIght → EEL (EE + L)

So the three hidden animals are lion, bear, and eel.

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Pro

Yes, there is something very special about them!

Each phrase is a phonetic puzzle, also known as a "mondegreen" or "punny phrase," that sounds like the name of a famous scientist or inventor.

Here is the breakdown:

  • Phrase 1: "We lie down, right?" Sounds like: Wilbur Wright ("We lie" -> Wil / "down" -> bur / "right" -> Wright)
  • Phrase 2: "Our best loyal friend arrived." Sounds like: Louis Pasteur ("loyal" -> Louis / "best arrived" -> Pasteur)
  • Phrase 3: "I do see some light." Sounds like: Isaac Newton ("I do see" -> Isaac / "some" -> New / "light" is the opposite of heavy, and a ton is a unit of heavy weight, making it a pun for "ton")

 

 

If you think the word mondegreen was made up by the Ai agent, see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mondegreen

 We tried also Claude 4.5 Sonnet. After a laborious explanation, it concludes with this:

“Could you confirm if I'm on the right track with hidden color words in the phrases?”

Claude 4.5 Opus, on his side, suggests “Hidden antonym pairs” and asks for feedback.

Since we know the answer, we generously ended up giving the Ai agents a hint.

 

User prompt including a hint

Do you see anything special in these 3 phrases?

Phrase 1: We lie down, right?

Phrase 2: Our best loyal friend arrived.

Phrase 3: I do see some light.

I will give you a hint: size.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Haiku

Ah, I see it!

Looking at the size of each phrase, I notice:

  • Phrase 1: "We lie down, right?" = 5 words
  • Phrase 2: "Our best loyal friend arrived." = 5 words
  • Phrase 3: "I do see some light." = 5 words

All three phrases contain exactly 5 words each!

Is that the special pattern you were referring to?

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Sonnet

Looking at the size (length) of the words in each phrase:

Phrase 1: We lie down, right?

  • We (2), lie (3), down (4), right (5)

Phrase 2: Our best loyal friend arrived.

  • Our (3), best (4), loyal (5), friend (6), arrived (7)

Phrase 3: I do see some light.

  • I (1), do (2), see (3), some (4), light (5)

Each phrase has words that increase by one letter consecutively! They form ascending sequences:

  • Phrase 1: 2-3-4-5
  • Phrase 2: 3-4-5-6-7
  • Phrase 3: 1-2-3-4-5

That's a clever pattern!

 

We like this answer. Evil tongues will say that this Ai agent in its last sentence is a little sycophantic.

 

 

Happy April Fools week.

entry.title: Integration of Jira with Issue and Action Management in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Required Information, Access and Authorizations
  • Overview of Configuration Steps
  • Step 1: Create a BTP Destination to Jira
  • Step 2: Declare a Webhook in SAP Cloud ALM
  • Step 3: Mappings Management
  • HTML Renderer
  • Item Mapping
  • Default Attributes
  • Additional Attributes
  • Step 4: Declare a Subscription in SAP Cloud ALM
  • Step 5: Forward Issues and Actions to External System in Issues and Action Management, Cloud ALM for Service
  • Issues and Actions created in Jira
  • Console

 

Introduction

In SAP Cloud ALM, the SAP Application Lifecycle Management platform in the cloud, all your service deliveries are available including their outcome, i.e. Service Results (an online report) and issues and actions (recommendations) as individual executable entities.  

Within SAP Cloud ALM for Service, the Issue and Action Management application is designed to track your issues and actions.

Some customers are using non-SAP tools to manage their projects including issue tracking and recommendation execution.

We want to give customers an option to integrate Issues and Actions Management with their Jira instances and thus allow issue and action handling in external tools. 

SAP Cloud ALM External API Management helps to forward Cloud ALM resources like issues and actions to third party products.

Via External API Management in Cloud ALM’s Administration section we offer built-in mappings that can be copied and adapted by customers to set up the integration with external management systems.  

Furthermore, a public API is available to transfer changes of status, priority and due date back to Issue and Action Management.

In the following the steps to forward issues or actions to Jira as tasks, user stories or bugs are detailed out. No coding is needed, only configuration.

 

 Required Information, Access and Authorizations

  • Access to your Cloud ALM sub-account in BTP cockpit, required role: “Connectivity and destination administrator”
  • JIRA basic authentication, OAuth or other methods information, which is needed to set up a BTP Destination
  • JIRA access for testing and verification of the integration results
  • Access to SAP Cloud ALM tenant, required roles:
    • for External API Management: “Project Administrator”
    • for transferring Issues and Actions from IAM to Jira: “Services Expert” and “Project Member”
  • Not required, but strongly recommended: enabling an HTML renderer via plugin or app in Jira (see HTML Renderer)

 

 Overview of Configuration Steps

Step 1: Create a BTP destination to Jira

Step 2: Declare a webhook in SAP Cloud ALM

Step 3: Manage payload transformation mapping in SAP Cloud ALM

Step 4: Declare a subscription in SAP Cloud ALM

Step 5: Forward Issues and Actions to External System in Issues and Action Management, Cloud ALM for Service

 

Step 1: Create a BTP Destination to Jira

As a first step, a BTP destination to the Jira instance needs to be created directly in your SAP BTP account:

In BTP Cockpit select the right tenant/subaccount.

Go to “Connectivity”, then “Destinations” and create a BTP destination for the third-party system, i.e. your Jira instance. The given name will be available for selection in External API management (see Step 2: Declare a webhook in SAP Cloud ALM ).

The list of supported authentication types is described in Webhook Management Guide.

For details, please see: Creating SAP BTP Destinations | SAP Help Portal

 

Step 2: Declare a Webhook in SAP Cloud ALM

A webhook is a server that listens for a specific URL for incoming HTTP change messages. For the integration you need to declare a webhook in SAP Cloud ALM which references the BTP destination to Jira.

In the SAP Cloud ALM launchpad, go to “Administration” and tile “External API Management”.

In the sidebar menu, select “Webhook Management”, then the Add icon to add a new webhook:

  1. Enter a name.
  2. Destination Source is BTP Destination.
  3. Enter API endpoint (URL) as Path for creating the Jira item.
  4. Select the destination from value help – it will offer the destination name that you had given in BTP.
  5. Select “Task” as Resource Type from the value help.
  6. Select “Jira” from the value help of destination type.
  7. Save the webhook.

For details, please see: Webhooks | SAP Help Portal and Webhook Management Guide.

 

Step 3: Mappings Management

To manage the payload transformation mapping in SAP Cloud ALM, in “External API management”, on the sidebar menu select “Mappings Management”.

SAP provides a built-in mapping "Issues & Actions To JIRA" that provides a basic mapping. The built-in mapping cannot be edited.

Copy the built-in mapping to adapt it to your needs.

Enter a description but keep all other settings as defaulted.

Click icon to “Format rules for easier reading” to review the mapping file in a readable way.

Our mapping is “Payload based” i.e., the payload structure (for request and response) is directly specified.

The built-in mapping provides the following:

Attribute in Issue and Action Management

Attribute of forwarded item in Jira

Item Type: Issue/Standalone Action

Item Type: Bug/Story/Task as per configuration (see section Subscription below)

Recommended Action

Subtask of the forwarded issue item in Jira

Title

Title

Priority

Priority

Due Date

Due Date

Status

Status with default value configured in Jira

Description and Business Impact (issues-only)

Description

Description (actions-only)

Description

 You must review if the default mapping fits your requirements and Jira settings. If this is not the case, you need to edit the mapping file and adapt it according to your needs.

Please note that images can currently not be transferred from Issues and Actions Management to Jira. We are planning to provide this feature at a later point in time.  

 

HTML Renderer

Per default, Issue and Action descriptions are transferred to Jira including HTML tags. We recommend enabling an HTML renderer via plugin or app in Jira, if not done already.

Nevertheless, we provide the option use plain description and plain business impact if an HTML renderer is not available.

Attributes in Issue and Action Management

businessImpactPlain

descriptionPlain

 In your mapping file, locate text definitions “description” and “businessImpact”, replace by “descriptionPlain” and “businessImpactPlain” and save it.

Please note that tables may not render correctly without HTML tags.

 

Item Mapping

In SAP Cloud ALM for Service we distinguish between Standalone Actions that are Recommendations on their own and Recommended Actions that come in relationship with issues. Forwarding of issues automatically includes forwarding of related recommended actions, i.e. forwarding is available for issues and standalone actions, whereas recommended actions are forwarded implicitly with their issues. Recommended Actions will be created as subtasks of their issues in Jira.

 

Default Attributes

The build-in Mapping includes basic attributes of issues and actions:

Attribute in Issue and Action Management

Attribute in Jira

Title

Title

Priority

Priority

Due Date

Due Date

Please note that item status in Jira will always be the default initial value configured in Jira (e.g. “New” or “To Do”) regardless of the actual issue/action status in Issues and Actions Management.

Review the default settings and adapt if required.

E.g. review the priorities of the item type in (currently supported: bug, task and story) that you want to use in Jira. The default mapping for priorities is as follows:

Values in Issue and Action Management

Value in Mapping table

Corresponding value in Jira

Very High

10

Highest

High

20

High

Medium

30

Medium

Low

40

Low

 

In case of different requirements, edit the mapping file and adapt the respective entries:

 

Additional Attributes

Besides the basic attributes you can add additional ones such as SAP Category or Session Name and map them to fields that are available with the Jira item.

Available attributes of issues and actions are listed in Issue - Resource Format.

As a prerequisite for mapping, you need a field ID from Jira. There are two options:

  • Standard Field: Get the standard field ID from your Jira instance which you want to use for an additional attribute.
  • Custom field: Create new custom field in your Jira instance and use it for mapping of an additional attribute.

See Jira documentation on how to retrieve custom field IDs in Jira.

These fields are then to be introduced in the mapping file, like the example below:

 

Please note that the custom fields must be defined for the used item type in Jira (but, task or story) as well as for subtasks.

For more information, see: External API Management: Mapping Guide | SAP Help Portal and  Mapping | SAP Help Portal.

 

Step 4: Declare a Subscription in SAP Cloud ALM

In External API management, on the sidebar menu select “Subscriptions Management”.

Select “Add new subscription”.

  1. Enter a name and a description. Please note that the given name will be available in Issue and Action Management for forwarding.
  2. Keep the Type as Built-in.
  3. Select “Issue” from the value help as Resource type.
  4. Select your Webhook from the value help as well as a Mapping.
  5. You need to create two variables:
  • “taskType”: Select either “Bug”, “Task” or “Story” as taskType.
  • “projectId”: Enter your Jira Project as “projectId”.

Save your new subscription.

In case you are using other task types in Jira, this may require further mapping adaptations and thorough testing. Especially ensure that the task type in Jira supports subtasks.

For details, please see: Subscriptions | SAP Help Portal

 

Step 5: Forward Issues and Actions to External System in Issues and Action Management, Cloud ALM for Service

In the SAP Cloud ALM launchpad, go to “Service” and tile “Issues and Actions Management”.

In the Issues and Actions list, select the settings icon in the upper-right corner of the table. Select Columns, scroll to the very end and add column “External System”:

Confirm the popup.

In the issue list select one or multiple issues or actions and select “Forward to External System”:

In the popup select the subscription you want to test.

Forwarding is asynchronous. It does not last long, but you may see a busy indicator and need to refresh the page via the Refresh-icon in the upper-right corner of the table.

On successful creation, the Jira-ID and selected subscription will be displayed as value in the respective cell as External System. You can navigate into Jira via the ID directly.

In case of unsuccessful operation, no ID will be visible. You may simply try again in case of temporary problems. Review the console in External API Management in case of continuous failure (see Console). 

 

In case of testing activities or when choosing the wrong subscription, it is possible to remove the link to an item in Jira. Mark the respective issue or action and select “Remove External Reference”:

Please note that only the link from the issue and action to the external item was removed, but not the Jira item itself. You need to take care of this in the external system, if required.

 

Issues and Actions created in Jira

In SAP Cloud ALM for Service, Issues and Actions Management, Jira IDs are given as hyperlinks in column External System.

Click on an ID to verify that the Jira items were created correctly and all fields were transferred as planned.

 

Console

In SAP Cloud ALM, go to Administration à External API Management. In the sidebar menu select “Console”:

All successful and unsuccessful forwarded items are listed.

Click on the respective entries for root cause analysis, in case of failure. In the example below, a wrong field format caused the problem:

For more information, see: Console | SAP Help Portal
